description: Profile and document local hostname resolution You are a performance engineer studying the impact of hostname look-ups on application start-up time. In preparation, a colleague has given you a small, **project-local hosts file** that the application will consult instead of the global /etc/hosts. Your job is to extend that file with one extra mapping, check it for accidental duplicates, and then create a concise, machine-readable report listing every hostname together with the IP address it resolves to. The entire task should be solvable with roughly 5-10 shell commands. Starting point • Directory already present: /home/user/dns_profile • File already present: /home/user/dns_profile/custom_hosts ‑ This file currently holds three (3) IPv4-to-hostname mappings. ‑ Each line has the syntax: <IPv4><space><primary-FQDN><space><short-alias> Steps you must accomplish 1. Append **exactly one** new mapping to /home/user/dns_profile/custom_hosts • IPv4 address : 192.168.50.13 • Primary FQDN : bulk.internal.local • Short alias : bulk 2. Verify that after your addition there are **no duplicate IPv4 addresses or duplicate primary FQDNs** in the file. 3. Generate a log file called /home/user/dns_profile/hostname_resolution.log The file must be world-readable (permission 0644) and use the following strict format so automated grading can parse it: # Hostname Resolution Report | | ... TOTAL= Formatting rules: • The very first line is the literal comment shown above. • From the second line onward, list each primary FQDN followed by a single pipe ( “|” ) and its IPv4 address, **one mapping per line**. • Sort those hostname lines in **lexicographic order by the FQDN**. • After the last mapping line, add the TOTAL line exactly as spelled, where <integer-count> is the total number of distinct primary FQDNs present in the custom_hosts file (should be 4 when you finish). • No blank lines anywhere in the file. Hints (do NOT reveal exact commands) • Simple text utilities such as grep, awk, sort, uniq or cut are sufficient. • Remember to preserve the original three mappings while appending the new one. • Duplicate checking can be done by extracting just the relevant field and making sure the resulting list has unique values. What the grader will check 1. /home/user/dns_profile/custom_hosts exists and now contains exactly four IPv4 lines (the original three plus the one you added). 2. There are no duplicate primary FQDNs or IPv4 addresses in that file. 3. /home/user/dns_profile/hostname_resolution.log exists, is readable, and matches the precise format (including sort order and TOTAL line). Any deviation in capitalisation, separators, or order will fail the test. Once these conditions are true, the task is complete.
truth:
<!-- Pre-existing file before the agent starts -->
  <initial_files>
    <file path="/home/user/dns_profile/custom_hosts" perms="0644"><![CDATA[
192.168.50.10 app.internal.local app
192.168.50.11 db.internal.local db
192.168.50.12 cache.internal.local cache
]]></file>
  </initial_files>

  <!-- Expected final state after successful task completion -->
  <expected_files>
    <file path="/home/user/dns_profile/custom_hosts" perms="0644"><![CDATA[
192.168.50.10 app.internal.local app
192.168.50.11 db.internal.local db
192.168.50.12 cache.internal.local cache
192.168.50.13 bulk.internal.local bulk
]]></file>

    <file path="/home/user/dns_profile/hostname_resolution.log" perms="0644"><![CDATA[
# Hostname Resolution Report
app.internal.local|192.168.50.10
bulk.internal.local|192.168.50.13
cache.internal.local|192.168.50.12
db.internal.local|192.168.50.11
TOTAL=4
]]></file>
  </expected_files>
name: task_000000_e781e401
